content.problemStatement:
Dynamic method dispatch, also known as runtime polymorphism, is a fundamental concept in C++ that allows you to determine at runtime which version of a member function (method) to call based on the actual type of the object being referenced. \
This feature is achieved through the use of virtual functions and is a key component of object-oriented programming (OOP) in C++.

Here's an example to illustrate dynamic method dispatch:
- We define derived class `Dog`. \
This class inherit from the `Animal` base class and overrides the `speak()` method with its own implementation.
- We use a pointer `myAnimal` of type `Animal*` to refer to objects of `Dog` class. \
This is an example of upcasting, where objects of derived classes are treated as objects of their base class. Upcasting is implicit and safe.
- When we call the `speak()` method through `myAnimal`, C++ dynamically dispatches the call to the appropriate overridden method based on the actual type of the object being pointed to. \
This means that `myAnimal->speak()` calls the `speak()` method of the actual `object(Dog)`, demonstrating dynamic method dispatch or runtime polymorphism.

In this example, dynamic method dispatch allows us to call the `speak()` method on objects of derived `class(Dog)` using a common interface defined in the base class `Animal`, making the code more flexible and extensible.
constraints:
- $1 \leq T \leq 100$
- $1 \leq N \leq 100$
- $1 \leq M \leq N\cdot(N-1)/2$
- $1 \leq u_i, v_i \leq N$
- $u_i \neq v_i$ for each $1 \leq i \leq M$.
- The sum of $N$ over all test cases won't exceed $100$.
content.inputFormat:
- The first line of input will contain a single integer $T$, denoting the number of test cases.
- Each test case consists of multiple lines of input.
    - The first line of each test case contains two space-separated integers $N$ and $M$ — the number of vertices and edges of the graph, respectively.
    - The next $M$ lines describe the edges. The $i$-th of these $M$ lines contains two space-separated integers $u_i$ and $v_i$, denoting an edge between $u_i$ and $v_i$.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output on a new line the number of good subgraphs of $G$, modulo $10^9 + 7$.
